images.caption: Figure 1:Comparison of static and dynamic (e.g., FAMA) agentic orchestration.

Figure 2:Overview of the FAMA framework.Stage 1:A baseline agent, without multi-agent assistance, is executed on domain tasks.Stage 2:failed tasks are analyzed and categorized by a human or an agentic framework.Stage 2.1:Independent agents focus on distinct issues and analyze the causes of failure through task-specific conversations.Stage 2.2:An orchestrator agent identifies the primary reasons for failure.Stage 2.3:A mitigation agent selects the most appropriate helper agents to address the identified issues.Stage 3:the baseline agent is re-executed using only the helper agents recommended by the mitigation agent.
Figure 3:Comparingτ\tau-bench performance across multiple LLMs being tested as user agents.

Figure 5:Statistics of recommendations produced by the Mitigation Agent across open source agents forτ\tau-bench.
Figure 6:Performance comparison of Qwen3-14B and Qwen3-32B (thinking variants) across methods onτ\tau-bench.

Figure 7:Token overhead (%) and latency (seconds) across different methods with Qwen3-32B. See Table2for detailed statistics. ‘ReAct-nt’ and ‘ReAct-t’ represent non-thinking and thinking models used for ReAct method.
Figure 8:Comparison of tool calling agents that rely on different agentic combinations.
